sections.summary: Identifies the 'Standard for Bananas CXS 205-1997', adopted in 1997 and amended in 2005 and 2022.

Records amendments made following the 45th Codex Alimentarius Commission (Dec 2022) and provides a table showing the textual revision to the 'Definition of produce' paragraph (original and printed text).

Defines scope: applies to commercial varieties of bananas grown from Musa spp. (Musaceae) in the green state to be supplied fresh after preparation and packaging, excludes bananas intended only for cooking (plantains) or industrial processing, with covered varieties listed in the annex.

Establishes quality provisions for bananas, comprising minimum condition/ development requirements (Section 2.1) and a three‑class classification (Extra, Class I, Class II) with class‑specific allowed defects and tolerances.

Specifies minimum requirements for bananas in all classes: they must be whole, sound (no rotting), clean, practically free of pests and pest damage, free of abnormal external moisture and foreign odours/tastes, firm, free of low‑temperature damage and significant bruising, without malformation or abnormal curvature, with pistils removed and intact, non‑desiccated stalks; hands/clusters must retain a sufficient, normally coloured, sound crown cleanly cut, and fruit development must permit reaching physiological maturity, withstanding transport/handling and arriving fit to ripen.
Defines three quality classes for bananas (Extra, Class I, Class II) and introduces the detailed criteria for each class.

'Extra' class requires bananas of superior quality, characteristic of the variety/commercial type, with fingers free of defects except very slight superficial faults that do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation.

Class I requires good quality bananas characteristic of the variety and allows only slight defects in shape and colour and slight skin defects from rubbing or superficial causes not exceeding 2 cm² of total surface area, provided these do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality, presentation or the flesh.

Class II covers bananas not qualifying for higher classes but meeting the minimum requirements and permits defects in shape and colour and skin defects (scraping, scabs, rubbing, blemishes) up to 4 cm² of total surface area, provided the fruit retains essential characteristics and the defects do not affect the flesh.

Defines sizing method for Gros Michel and Cavendish subgroup bananas: length measured along the outside curve from blossom end to base of pedicel and diameter as transverse thickness, using the median outer row finger for hands and the finger adjacent to the cut for clusters. Sets minimums: length not less than 14 cm and minimum grade (diameter) not less than 2.7 cm.

Specifies tolerances by class for quality defects: Extra class may include up to 5% (number or weight) not meeting Extra but meeting Class I, Class I up to 10% not meeting Class I but meeting Class II, and Class II up to 10% not meeting the class or minimum requirements except for produce unfit for consumption; size tolerances allow 10% (number or weight) outside the indicated size but within the immediately adjacent size above or below.

Sets quality tolerances for bananas by class: Extra allows up to 5% (by number or weight) not meeting Extra requirements but meeting Class I (or exceptionally within Class I tolerances); Class I allows up to 10% not meeting its requirements but meeting Class II (or exceptionally within Class II tolerances); Class II allows up to 10% that fail both the class and minimum requirements, excluding produce unfit for consumption due to rotting, major imperfections or similar deterioration.
For the 'Extra' class, up to 5% by number or weight of bananas may fail to meet Extra requirements provided they meet Class I requirements or, exceptionally, fall within Class I tolerances.
For Class I, up to 10% by number or weight of bananas may fail to meet Class I requirements provided they meet Class II requirements or, exceptionally, fall within Class II tolerances.

For Class II, up to 10% by number or weight of bananas may fail to meet both the class requirements and the minimum requirements, except that produce rendered unfit for consumption by rotting, major imperfections or similar deterioration is excluded.

For all classes, up to 10% by number or weight of bananas may not satisfy the specified sizing requirements provided they fall within the size immediately above or below the sizes indicated in Section 3.

Requires packaged bananas to be uniform in origin, variety and quality with the visible contents representative of the whole; they must be packed to protect the produce using new, clean (or food-grade recycled) materials that do not cause damage and only non-toxic inks/glues for any printing, and containers must meet hygiene, ventilation and resistance standards. Presentation rules specify bananas may be packed in hands, clusters (parts of hands) of at least four fingers or as single fingers, allow clusters with up to two missing fingers if the stalk is cleanly cut, and limit one three-finger cluster per row under the same characteristics as other fruit in the package.
Each package must be uniform and contain only bananas of the same origin, variety and quality, and the visible portion of the package must be representative of the entire contents.

Bananas must be packed to protect them, using inner materials that are new, clean or food-grade recycled and of quality that avoids external or internal damage, with any printed trade specifications using non-toxic ink or glue, and packaging must comply with the Code of Practice for Packaging and Transport of Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 44-1995).

Containers for bananas must meet specified quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance characteristics to ensure proper handling, shipping and preservation, and packages must be free of foreign matter and odour.

Bananas must be presented in hands and clusters (parts of hands) of at least four fingers or as single fingers; clusters with up to two missing fingers are allowed if the stalk is cleanly cut and not torn, and no more than one three‑finger cluster with the same characteristics may appear per row.

General marking and labelling provisions for bananas: consumer packages are subject to the General Standard for the Labelling of Pre‑packaged Foods (CXS 11985) plus additional specific provisions, and non‑retail containers must bear specified particulars legibly and indelibly on the same side or in accompanying shipment documents.
Consumer packages of bananas must comply with the General Standard for the Labelling of Pre‑packaged Foods (CXS 11985) in addition to the specific provisions given here.
If bananas (the produce) are not visible from the outside, each consumer package must be labelled with the name of the produce and may also indicate the variety.

Non‑retail containers of bananas must bear specified particulars grouped on the same side, legibly and indelibly marked and visible from the outside, or these particulars must appear in the shipment documents.
Non‑retail banana packages must show the name and address of the exporter, packer and/or dispatcher, and may include an identification code.
For non‑retail banana packages, the name of the produce must be shown if contents are not visible, and the name of the variety or commercial type may be included.
Requires labelling the country of origin of the produce and optionally the district or other national, regional or local place name.
Commercial identification must indicate product presentation (e.g., bananas in fingers when appropriate), the class, and may optionally show net weight.
Only a heading noting that an official inspection mark is optional; no substantive requirement is provided.
The produce covered by this standard (bananas) must comply with Codex maximum levels for contaminants and toxins (CXS 193-1995) and with Codex maximum residue limits for pesticides.

Recommends that the produce covered by this standard be prepared and handled pursuant to the General Principles of Food Hygiene, the Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables and other relevant Codex texts; the produce should also comply with any microbiological criteria established in accordance with CXG 21-1997.

sections.section: 8.1 It is recommended that the produce covered by the provisions of this standard be prepared and handled in accordance with the appropriate sections of the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969), 4 Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 53-2003), 5  and other relevant Codex texts such as codes of hygienic practice and codes of practice.
sections.summary: Recommends preparing and handling the produce in accordance with the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969), the Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 53-2003) and other relevant Codex texts.

sections.section: 8.2 The produce should comply with any microbiological criteria established in accordance with the Principles and Guidelines for the Establishment and Application of Microbiological Criteria Related to Foods (CXG 21-1997).
sections.summary: States that the produce should comply with any microbiological criteria established pursuant to the Principles and Guidelines for the Establishment and Application of Microbiological Criteria Related to Foods (CXG 21-1997).
Annex listing the main banana groups, subgroups and example dessert cultivars (e.g., AA, AB, AAA, AAB) with representative cultivar names for each subgroup.

sections.section: LIST OF THE MAIN GROUPS, SUBGROUPS AND CULTIVARS OF BANANAS FOR DESSERT
sections.summary: Substantive table listing the main taxonomic groups, subgroups and example cultivars of dessert bananas (e.g., AA Sweet‑fig: Sweet‑fig, Pisang Mas; AB Ney Poovan: Ney Poovan; multiple AAA subgroups including Cavendish, Gros Michel and Pink Fig with example cultivars; AAB subgroups such as Apple Fig, Pome, Mysore).
